1: ki-yaqum beqirBEKHA navi o ḥolam ḥalom (kî-yāqûm bĕqirbĕkā nābîʾ ʾô ḥōlēm ḥălôm): 'If a prophet or a dreamer of dreams arises among you.' Chapter 13 deals with three scenarios of enticement to apostasy. nābîʾ (prophet) and ḥōlēm ḥălôm (dreamer of dreams): the two recognized channels of divine communication in Israel's prophetic tradition. The test case is deliberately extreme — what if the sign actually comes to pass?
2: venatan eleicha ot o mofet (wĕnātan ʾēlêkā ʾôt ʾô mōp̄ēt): 'And the sign or wonder comes to pass.' ʾôt (sign) and mōp̄ēt (wonder, portent): the same sign-wonder pair from the exodus (Deuteronomy 4:34; Deuteronomy 6:22; Deuteronomy 7:19; Deuteronomy 26:8) — but here fulfilled by a false prophet. The scenario is shocking: even verified predictive ability does not authenticate the prophet if he directs Israel toward other gods. Accuracy is necessary but not sufficient for prophetic authority — the content determines the verdict.
3: lo tishmaʿ el-divrei hanavi hahu (lōʾ tišmaʿ ʾel-dibrê hannābîʾ hahûʾ): 'Do not listen to the words of that prophet.' The command is absolute — regardless of the sign's fulfillment. ki menasseh YHWH eloheichem etchem (for YHWH your God is testing you): the false prophet who produces genuine signs is permitted by YHWH as a nissāyôn (test, trial) — to reveal whether Israel's love for YHWH is absolute or contingent on miraculous display. Matthew 24:24 and Revelation 13:13-14 use the same logic: false prophets will produce great signs to deceive.
4: acharei YHWH eloheichem telchu: 'Follow the LORD your God.' The five verbs of total covenant loyalty: hālak ʾaḥărê (follow after), yārēʾ (fear), šāmar miṣwōt (keep commandments), šāmaʿ bĕqōlô (obey his voice), ʿābaḏ (serve), dābaq (hold fast). The five-fold response is the proper orientation that the false prophet's enticement tests. The test reveals whether this comprehensive loyalty is genuine or merely formal.
5: veHAnavi hahu o ḥolam haḥalom hahu yumat (wĕhannābîʾ hahûʾ ʾô ḥōlēm haḥălôm hahûʾ yûmāt): 'That prophet or dreamer shall be put to death.' The death penalty for the false prophet — regardless of whether his signs came true. kî dibber-sārâ ʿal-Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkem (for he has spoken rebellion against YHWH your God): sārâ (rebellion, apostasy) — the technical term for the covenant treason that warrants the most severe penalty. ûbiʿartā hārāʿ miqqirbĕkā (purge the evil from your midst): the biʿēr (purge/burn out) formula that runs through Deuteronomy's penal code (Deuteronomy 17:7,12; Deuteronomy 19:13,19; Deuteronomy 21:21; Deuteronomy 22:21-24; Deuteronomy 24:7).
6: ki yesitcha acheicha ben-immecha (kî yĕsîtĕkā ʾāḥîkā ben-ʾimmekā): 'If your brother, the son of your mother, entices you.' The second scenario: the intimate enticer — family member (brother, son, daughter, wife, close friend). mēsît (enticer): from sût (to incite, persuade, entice) — the same root used for Satan enticing David (1 Chronicles 21:1) and YHWH testing Job (Job 2:3). The social proximity makes the enticement most dangerous — it comes wrapped in personal loyalty and love.
7: nelcha venavda elohim aḥerim: “Let us go and serve other gods.” The content of the enticement: gods of the surrounding peoples — mēʾĕlōhê hāʿammîm ʾăšer sĕbîbōtêkem (from the gods of the peoples surrounding you), near or far, unknown. The anonymity of the suggested gods ('which neither you nor your fathers have known') emphasizes their lack of covenant history — no basis for loyalty except the enticer's relationship with you.
8: lo-tove lo velo tishmaʿ elav: 'Do not yield to him or listen to him.' The four prohibitions toward the intimate enticer: lōʾ-ʾābeh (do not yield), lōʾ-tišmaʿ (do not listen), lōʾ-tāḥôs ʿênĕkā (do not have pity), lōʾ tĕḥuml (do not show compassion), lōʾ tĕkassē ʿālāyw (do not conceal him). The cascade of negatives describes the total severance of relationship loyalty where covenant loyalty is at stake. The law is radical and deliberately shocking — it requires placing YHWH above even the most intimate human bonds.
9: ki harog tahargennu (kî hārōg tahargennû): 'You shall surely kill him.' The intensified double construction (hārōg tahargennû — infinitive absolute + finite verb) demands the death penalty. yādĕkā tihyeh bô bāriʾšōnâ lĕhāmîtô (your hand shall be first against him to put him to death): the accuser executes first. The public execution and the subsequent lapidation (rāgam bāʾăbānîm) witnesses the community's covenant solidarity.
10: usekaltho baavanim vaMET: 'Stone him to death.' ûsĕqaltô bāʾăbānîm wāmēt: the stoning is the community's collective act — the mob-execution that distributes responsibility across the whole body. kî biqqēš lĕhadd ōtĕkā mēʿal Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā (for he sought to drive you from YHWH your God): niddaḥ (to drive away, banish): the enticement's goal is covenant expulsion — separation from the relationship that alone gives life.
11: vechal-Yisrael yishmeʿu veyireʾu: 'All Israel shall hear and fear.' yišmĕʿû wĕyîrĕʾû (shall hear and fear): the public execution's deterrent function. The execution's publicity transforms individual punishment into communal instruction. wĕlōʾ-yōsîp̄û laʿăśôt kaddābār hārāʿ hazzeh bĕqirbĕkā (shall no longer do such evil in your midst): the deterrence logic of Deuteronomy's penal code — the punishment prevents the replication of the crime.
12: ki-tishmaʿ beachat areicah asher YHWH eloheicha noten lecha: 'If you hear in one of your cities that YHWH is giving you.' The third scenario: the devoted city (ʿîr hanniddaḥat). The scope expands from individual false prophet (Deuteronomy 13:1-5) to individual enticer (Deuteronomy 13:6-11) to an entire city that has embraced apostasy. The city-scale apostasy is the maximal community test.
13: yatzeʾu anashim bĕnei-veliyaʿal (yāṣĕʾû ʾănāšîm bĕnê-bĕliyyaʿal): 'Worthless men have gone out from among you.' bĕnê bĕliyyaʿal (sons of Belial/worthlessness): bĕliyyaʿal = bĕlî (without) + yaʿal (profit/worth) — 'men of no worth.' In later usage Belial becomes a name for Satan (2 Corinthians 6:15). midduchê (enticement): the same root as niddaḥ (driven away) — the worthless men push (dāḥâ) the city toward other gods.
14: vedarashta vehiqarta vechalqarta heitev: 'Inquire, investigate, and question thoroughly.' The judicial process for the city charge: dāraš (inquire, seek out), ḥāqar (investigate), šāʾal (ask, question) — all in the piel/intensive form. hêṭēb (thoroughly, well): the thorough-investigation requirement protects against mob justice. The same investigative standard applies to individual capital cases (Deuteronomy 17:4; Deuteronomy 19:18).
15: hakov takov et-yoshevei hair hahi lefi-ḥarev: 'Strike the inhabitants of that city with the edge of the sword.' If the investigation confirms apostasy, the city receives the ḥērem (devoted-thing ban). ḥāram taḥărîm ʾōtāh: the double infinitive absolute (ḥāram taḥărîm) marks the totality of the destruction. The devoted city becomes like Canaanite cities under the ban — the apostasy of an Israelite city is the functional equivalent of the Canaanite opposition to YHWH.
16: veet-kol-shelala tiqbots el-toch rechova: 'Gather all its spoil into the middle of its open square.' rĕḥōb (open square, broad place): the central public space. The spoil (šālāl) and the city itself are burned as a ḥērem to YHWH (kālîl laYhwh ʾĕlōhêkā — a whole offering to YHWH). Unlike normal war plunder which is distributed, the devoted-city's wealth returns entirely to YHWH — it cannot enrich Israel. The city is never to be rebuilt.
17: veLo-yidbaq beyadcha meuma min-haḥerem: 'Let nothing devoted remain in your hand.' dābaq (cling, adhere): the same hold-fast verb used positively for covenant loyalty (Deuteronomy 13:4; Deuteronomy 11:22) — here the negative: nothing of the ḥērem shall cling to you. Achan's sin in Joshua 7:1-26 is the enacted violation of this principle — one man's taking from the devoted things brings defeat on the whole community. The devoted-city's total destruction is the purity required for YHWH to 'turn from his fierce anger' (šûb mēḥărôn ʾappô).
18: ki tishmaʿ beqol YHWH eloheicha: 'When you obey the voice of YHWH your God.' The closing covenant formula: obedience (šāmaʿ bĕqôl) to all the commandments produces YHWH's mercy (raḥămîm) and compassion and multiplication. laʿăśôt hayyāšār bĕʿênê Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā (doing what is right in the eyes of YHWH your God): the positive form of the formula that Deuteronomy 13:8 condemned in its chaotic negative version. The chapter that began with the false prophet test closes with the promise of covenant restoration through obedience.
